content.text: What Makes a Runbook Operationally Complete

A runbook is only useful when an on-call engineer — possibly unfamiliar with the specific service — can pick it up at 3 AM and resolve an incident within a defined time window. Completeness is not about length; it is about having every piece of information needed, in the right order, with no dead ends. An incomplete runbook for a GenAI system is especially costly because quality regressions and safety incidents evolve faster than most infrastructure failures and require domain-specific knowledge to interpret.
The completeness criteria below apply to any operational runbook, with GenAI-specific extensions for the metric categories and rollback targets unique to model-serving systems.

attrs.alt: Runbook anatomy — required sections and GenAI-specific notes

content.text: Every runbook must declare who owns it, who is the backup contact, the last date it was reviewed, and the severity class it addresses. For GenAI services, include the LLM provider, active model version, and the feature flag name that gates the feature. Without this metadata, a runbook becomes an orphan that no one updates.
Scopes which codebase owns the fix
Primary contact, not a team alias
Must be within 90 days for CI to pass
Flag to disable for instant rollback
Pinned version for model rollback target

The symptoms section describes what an engineer observes that causes them to open this runbook. It must include the exact alert name and the condition that fires it, the dashboard path and panel name, and the pattern of user-visible impact. Ambiguous symptom descriptions cause the wrong runbook to be used.
Copy the exact alert label from Alertmanager, not a paraphrase.
Grafana URL with dashboard ID and panel name so the engineer can visualize in one click.
What support tickets look like. For GenAI, this is often 'answers are wrong' or 'responses are truncated', not a hard error.
Other metrics that move together. An eval drop often co-occurs with latency increase when a larger model is misconfigured.

Impact tells the engineer how serious this is before they start investigating. It states: the scope of affected users, whether an SLO is at risk, and any downstream systems that depend on this service. For GenAI systems, note that quality degradation is invisible to error dashboards because the model still returns HTTP 200. Impact must be quantified through eval metrics, not error rates.

Diagnosis is the most commonly incomplete section. It must be executable: every step should be a copy-paste command or a specific dashboard path, never a general instruction like 'check the logs'. The sequence should match the order a rational engineer would investigate: confirm the metric, narrow the time window, find correlated events (deploys, config changes, upstream model changes).

Mitigation and rollback are distinct. Mitigation reduces blast radius immediately — traffic shifting, rate limiting, fallback mode — without necessarily resolving the root cause. Rollback is the full revert. Separating them matters because some incidents are best mitigated first to reduce user impact, then investigated before a full rollback decision.

For GenAI services, the fastest mitigation is almost always the feature flag. Disabling it moves all traffic to the fallback path within seconds. Mitigation steps must be tried before a full service rollback whenever the flag is available.
The rollback section must list every available rollback target, from fastest to most disruptive, with the expected time-to-recovery and the verification command for each:
Each verification command must produce observable output. Waiting and watching is not a verification step.

Pre-written templates remove cognitive load during an incident and prevent improvised messages that either under- or over-communicate severity. The runbook must specify who to notify, at which severity, which channel to use, and the exact message template.

Every runbook must end with a post-incident checklist to close the resolution loop. For GenAI services, this always includes: exporting the eval time-series for the incident window, adding any new failure patterns to the regression eval suite, reviewing whether the model pin policy needs updating, and filing the post-incident review within the agreed SLA.
A GenAI service requires at minimum one runbook per category of operational failure. The following table defines the minimum required set:
Example: Eval Pass Rate Degradation Runbook
The following is a reference implementation following the eight-section structure:

# Runbook: Eval Pass Rate Degradation
service: genai-answer-service
owner: @ml-platform
backup: @sre-oncall
severity: P2
last_reviewed: 2026-06-01
feature_flag: genai_answer_v2
model_version: gpt-4o-2024-08-06

## Overview
Triggered when the 7-day rolling eval pass rate drops below 94%.
Indicates quality regression in LLM responses.

## Symptoms
- Alert: EvalPassRateDegradation (threshold < 0.94 for 30m)
- Dashboard: grafana/d/genai-quality -> 'Eval Pass Rate 7d'
- User reports: support tickets mentioning wrong or irrelevant answers

## Impact
- Users receive lower-quality AI responses
- Trust erosion; potential SLO breach (eval >= 94% is contracted)
- No data loss, no safety risk (covered by separate runbook)

## Diagnosis
```bash
# 1. Confirm the drop
promtool query instant 'avg_over_time(eval_pass_rate[7d])'

# 2. Identify when it started
promtool query range --start='-48h' 'eval_pass_rate'

# 3. Check for recent deploys
kubectl rollout history deploy/genai-answer

# 4. Check current prompt version
kubectl get cm genai-prompt-v2 -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}'
```

## Mitigation
If the drop correlates with a recent deploy or prompt change:
1. Increase eval sampling rate to 100% to get faster signal
2. Pin traffic to the previous prompt template (see Rollback)
3. Do NOT raise the SLO threshold to silence the alert

## Rollback
```bash
# Option A: feature flag (fastest, <30s)
launchdarkly --project genai --env production disable genai_answer_v2

# Option B: image rollback (if flag not available)
kubectl rollout undo deploy/genai-answer
kubectl rollout status deploy/genai-answer  # wait for Ready

# Verify recovery
promtool query instant 'eval_pass_rate'  # expect recovery within 5m
```

## Communications
- Severity P2: page @ml-platform and notify #genai-ops
- Template: 'Quality degradation on genai-answer. Eval at X%% (SLO: 94%%).'
- Do not post in public channels until root cause is confirmed

## Post-Incident
- [ ] Export eval time-series for the incident window
- [ ] Add failing prompt pattern to regression eval suite
- [ ] File ticket to update eval baseline if model was upgraded
- [ ] Complete PIR at https://wiki/pir-template within 3 business days
Completeness is enforced automatically in CI. The audit script checks that every runbook has all eight required sections and has been reviewed within 90 days:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# scripts/audit_runbooks.py — check runbook completeness
import os, json, datetime

REQUIRED_SECTIONS = [
    '## Overview', '## Symptoms', '## Impact',
    '## Diagnosis', '## Mitigation', '## Rollback',
    '## Communications', '## Post-Incident',
]

def audit_runbook(path):
    text = open(path).read()
    missing = [s for s in REQUIRED_SECTIONS if s not in text]
    reviewed = None
    for line in text.splitlines():
        if line.startswith('last_reviewed:'):
            reviewed = datetime.date.fromisoformat(line.split()[-1])
            break
    stale = None
    if reviewed:
        age = (datetime.date.today() - reviewed).days
        stale = age > 90
    return {
        'file': path,
        'missing_sections': missing,
        'stale': stale,
        'pass': not missing and stale is False,
    }

if __name__ == '__main__':
    runbooks = [f for f in os.listdir('runbooks/') if f.endswith('.md')]
    results = [audit_runbook(f'runbooks/{rb}') for rb in runbooks]
    failing = [r for r in results if not r['pass']]
    print(json.dumps(failing, indent=2))
    raise SystemExit(len(failing))
Every Prometheus alert must carry a runbook_url annotation pointing to the correct runbook file. The following script verifies this linkage in CI:
The corresponding alert definition includes the annotation:

groups:
  - name: genai.quality
    rules:
      - alert: EvalPassRateDegradation
        expr: avg_over_time(eval_pass_rate[7d]) < 0.94
        for: 30m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          service: genai-answer
        annotations:
          summary: 'Eval pass rate below SLO'
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki.internal/runbooks/eval-pass-rate-degradation.md'
      - alert: SafetyFlagRateElevated
        expr: |
          rate(safety_flag_total[5m]) /
          rate(llm_request_total[5m]) > 0.0005
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: 'Safety flag rate above threshold'
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki.internal/runbooks/safety-flag-rate-elevated.md'

Every runbook has a declared owner responsible for keeping it current. Ownership transfers with service ownership — when a team inherits a service, they review and re-sign all runbooks within 30 days. Beyond scheduled 90-day reviews, a runbook must be updated immediately after:
Any incident that exposed a gap in the runbook steps
A model version upgrade that changes latency or output characteristics
A prompt template change that affects the eval metric baselines
A change to the LLM provider or fallback path
A feature flag rename or deprecation
The review scheduler scans all runbooks and opens Jira tickets for overdue reviews, integrating with sprint planning:
Completeness Gate in the Launch Checklist
Runbook completeness is a hard launch gate. A GenAI service cannot proceed to production without passing the automated audit and obtaining a human sign-off from the on-call rotation.
All 8 sections present in every runbook
Every alert has a runbook_url annotation
On-call can locate each runbook in <2 min
All rollback commands tested in staging
Comms templates approved by comms team
A runbook that has never been executed is a hypothesis, not a procedure. Every runbook must be walked through in a drill before it is trusted in a live incident.
Stale model version in rollback step
Commands that only work for the author
Owner who has left the team

Operational runbook completeness for GenAI services requires eight sections in every document: a header with ownership and model metadata, observable symptoms mapped to specific alerts, impact quantified through eval and quality metrics, executable diagnosis steps, distinct mitigation and rollback procedures ordered by speed, communications templates with stakeholder routing by severity, and a post-incident checklist that feeds back into the eval suite. Completeness is enforced through CI scripts that audit section coverage, staleness, and alert-to-runbook linkage. No GenAI service should enter production without every runbook passing the automated completeness gate and having been exercised in a drill.
